The later singles were better than forcing, but I do not want “base hits” to quietly become overtrading by another name.
journal.lessonLearned: Passing the knife catch was the right win, even if a risky bounce would have worked for a moment. The better long is still momentum shift plus pullback hold. But on a downtrending day, even good longs should often be managed like rentals. If price reaches the 10-minute 8 EMA or other real resistance without clear expansion, 1R is enough. Good entry plus realistic management beats good entry plus ambitious hope.
journal.nextRule: In a downtrending market, treat longs as rentals until proven otherwise. No falling-knife buys. Wait for momentum shift and pullback hold, and if price reaches the first real confluence resistance without strong expansion, take the 1R.

journal.title: Trading Journal — Wednesday, April 22, 2026
journal.mood: Much calmer and more structured than last week. There was still annoyance around break-even and missed continuation, but the overall feel of the day was more mature: better entries, less impulse, and more awareness of when old slot-machine energy was trying to sneak back in.
journal.setupContext: Today reinforced how much better the newer long model is working for me. The best entries came after structure improved, not from buying the flush itself. Support tests, reclaim holds, and continuation pullbacks were much cleaner than anything based on hope. I also got a live reminder that once a move becomes too extended, missing it is still better than chasing it. The trade-off now is not finding entries. It is managing good entries well, especially when price reaches obvious decision zones like 27000 or the 10-minute 8 EMA and starts hesitating.
journal.execution: Trade 1: Long 1 MNQM6 from 26852.50 to 26887.25 for +$69.50. Trade 2: Long 1 from 26900.25 to 26930.75 for +$61.00. Trade 3: Long 1 from 26976.25 to 26977.25 for +$2.00 after moving to break-even protection and getting shaken out. Trade 4: Long 1 from 27034.00 to 27037.50 for +$7.00. Trade 5: Long 1 from 27042.25 to 27030.00 for -$24.50. Trade 6: Long 1 from 27045.00 to 27050.75 for +$11.50. Total gross: +$126.50. The first two trades were the cleanest examples of how much entry quality has improved. The third trade carried the most emotional weight because it was a good setup that got protected at break-even after a real rejection, then later ripped without me.
journal.psychology: Recovery Metrics: good. I felt the normal frustration after the break-even stop and the missed follow-through, but I caught the exact moment old-me wanted to hop right back on just because the market kept going. I named it as slot-machine mode and did not obey it. That was a meaningful win. I also handled the later “missed the boat” feeling better by recognizing extension instead of using frustration as permission to chase.
journal.ruleAdherence: Good overall. I stayed at 1 contract and respected the no-chase rule better. I passed the late extended moves instead of trying to force a top-of-burst entry. I also ended the day intentionally instead of turning solid progress into overtrading. The main refinement area is management, not entries. Break-even and doji-based caution were understandable in context, but I still need better rules for when to pay partial into first resistance and when to let a winner breathe one more candle before deciding.
journal.whatWentWell: My entries were genuinely strong today. They were proof-based, structured, and much better than last week’s style. I also showed real awareness of emotional danger when the urge to re-enter after a frustrating BE showed up. Another positive was correctly recognizing when a move had become too extended to chase, even if that meant watching it continue without me.
journal.whatToImprove: I need cleaner management rules for good trades that run into decision areas. A doji by itself is not the signal. Context matters: resistance, acceptance, rejection, and what the next candle does. I also want a more consistent plan for partials versus BE so I do not keep ending up in the most annoying version of the outcome where the structure warns me, I protect correctly, and then the market shakeouts before continuing.
journal.lessonLearned: The edge is shifting from entries to management. That is a good problem. Today proved my entries can be strong when I wait for reclaim, higher low, and pullback hold. The next improvement is learning how to handle hesitation at key zones without either suffocating the trade too early or giving the market too much benefit of the doubt. Also, resisting the immediate re-entry impulse after a frustrating BE is real progress, not a small detail.
journal.nextRule: Keep the proof-based entries. For management, treat doji as a yellow light, not an automatic exit. At key resistance, either pay partial and protect the rest, or wait one more candle for true failure versus acceptance. And if a move becomes extended after I miss it, no chasing, just wait for the next real setup.

journal.title: Trading Journal — Thursday, April 23, 2026
journal.mood: Mixed but productive. Early in the day I felt sharper and more structured, and later I could feel the emotional side trying to interfere through stop movement, repair trading thoughts, and the need to make the day feel fair. The positive is that I caught a lot of those dynamics in real time instead of blindly obeying them.
journal.setupContext: The day reinforced several setup rules. When I mixed timeframes, like seeing green candles on the 1-minute and only later realizing the broader picture was still messy, the trade got harder to manage. The cleaner version was when the higher timeframe bias and lower timeframe trigger matched, especially breakout-pullback-hold or clear reclaim structure. I also got more evidence that entering inside consolidation is harder for me than waiting for breakout, then pullback and hold. The market repeatedly showed that “buyers might step in” is not enough by itself; I want proof, room, and a defined invalidation before clicking.
journal.execution: Trade 1: +$12.50. Trade 2: +$28.00. Trade 3: +$5.50. Trade 4: +$68.00 on a clean support-to-resistance trade from 27015.00 to 27049.00, one of the better examples of structured execution today. Trade 5: -$74.50 from 27091.00 to 27053.75 after I broke the rule and moved the stop to the previous low under a “maybe liquidity sweep” idea instead of honoring the original risk. Trade 6: +$58.50 from 27057.00 to 27086.25 on a defensible buyers-step-in reversal, though the decision quality was still somewhat downgraded by the emotional context after the previous loss. Trade 7: -$0.50 from 27090.50 to 27090.25 on the long hold-practice trade. This was the most useful management rep of the day: in the future 2-contract model, first piece would come off at 1R, runner would move to BE, then the runner would scratch when quality degraded. Trade 8: +$1.00. Trade 9: -$50.00 from 26913.50 to 26888.50. Trade 10: -$50.00 from 26875.25 to 26850.25. Trade 11: +$77.50 from 26896.25 to 26935.00 on the late buyers-step-in move that had a real chart reason, even though my state around it was not perfectly clean. Total gross: +$76.00.
journal.psychology: Recovery Metrics: mixed. The biggest win was not P&L, it was awareness. I explicitly noticed that every time I let the emotional side drive, I lost quality. That showed up in the stop-widening rule break, and later in the thought process of wanting to “pay myself back” after earlier frustration. I also had a genuinely healthy rep on holding through discomfort. On the 27090.50 long, I practiced holding because structure had not broken, not because I needed 2R to feel better. When the trade weakened and a future-runner BE would have been hit, I could see the model clearly. That is valuable emotional training, even though the runner itself did not work.
journal.ruleAdherence: Mixed. Strong on many entries, especially the proof-based longs and the no-trade discipline around obvious knife-catching moments. Weak on management when I moved the stop after entry to the previous low. That is still a hard rule break and cannot be normalized. I also took more trades than ideal, which means state management still needs work. On the positive side, I did a much better job than older me at naming compromised-state thinking instead of pretending emotion was not involved.
journal.whatWentWell: Entries continue to improve. I identified better reclaim and pullback-hold structures, handled some resistance-based exits well, and used the 2-contract simulation lens productively. The biggest single process win was learning what the runner model actually feels like in real time: first piece pays, runner gets a chance, BE protects capital when quality degrades, and not every runner becomes a big second leg. I also did a better job spotting that the emotional side always produces worse trading when it gets authority.
journal.whatToImprove: No post-entry stop changes. If the previous low is the real invalidation, it has to be planned before entry, not invented after discomfort starts. I also need to tighten the rule around compromised-state trades: even if a chart trigger is real, it should be skipped if the motive includes fairness, repair, or needing to get paid back after a frustrating prior trade. Finally, I want to reduce the total number of trades. More reps are useful, but too many decisions create too many openings for the emotional side to start negotiating.
journal.lessonLearned: The emotional side is a bad trader. It can sound reasonable, but when it starts placing orders or changing risk, quality drops fast. By contrast, the structured side can live with clean losses and clean scratches because the trade either earned the next step or it did not. Today also proved that holding through discomfort is worth practicing, but only when the hold is tied to structure rather than hope. The future 2-contract model makes sense: 1R off on the first piece, runner to BE by default, and then let the chart decide whether there is a real second leg.
journal.nextRule: No changing the stop after entry. If a trade reaches 1R and is a true runner candidate, first piece comes off there and the runner goes to BE by default. If the reason for the next trade includes “I need to get paid back,” “let me just make this one count,” or any version of emotional repair, do not take it.

journal.title: Trading Journal — Friday, April 24, 2026
journal.mood: Hijacked, frustrated, and reactive for too much of the session. I could feel the old version of me coming back with force after the first 1R reversal, and once that emotional wound opened, decision quality kept degrading.
journal.setupContext: The chart lesson is clearer now: 10-minute trend and momentum state need to own the management plan. Early in the day, when the 10-minute context was still trending down, the better long framework should have been scalp mentality and take 1R without over-negotiating. Later, if the 10-minute chart truly shifted and held, then a 1R first target with room for 2R+ would make sense. Instead, I kept getting pulled into 1-minute urgency, then reacted to reversals emotionally when those quick decisions did not pay the way I wanted.
journal.execution: There were 24 trades today, which by itself says too much state leakage got into the session. Early sequence: -$52.50, -$50.50, -$51.00, -$50.00, -$50.50 before a few tiny winners. Later I had several small wins and one decent recovery trade, but the big pattern never changed: hit or approach 1R, feel the emotional weight of wanting more or wanting repair, then either lose the clean management or revenge-click the next setup. A later long that hit 1R and reversed to stop was structurally defensible as a hold attempt because it had real support from hammer/doji behavior, VWAP, and 10-minute 8 EMA context. The actual problem came after it failed, not before. After that stopout, confidence dropped, revenge trades started, and one later V-shape recovery after my stop made the emotional damage even worse. Total gross: -$183.50.
journal.psychology: Recovery Metrics: poor. The emotional side clearly got authority. I could hear the thoughts: we could have had 1R, we need to make it back, this next one can fix the day. Once that voice was in charge, the next trades were not clean decisions, they were attempts to repair feeling and fairness. The one positive is that I named it accurately in real time. I knew I was emotionally hijacked. I also finally said the deeper truth out loud: every time I listen to the emotional side and act on it, I lose. That is painful but valuable clarity.
journal.ruleAdherence: Weak. Trade count alone shows that. I kept trading after recognizing emotional hijack. I took revenge trades. I let the day turn into repair trading instead of shutting it down sooner. The better rule-following moments were there, but I did not protect them. The strongest rules I broke were state-management rules, not just technical-entry rules. Once I knew I was hijacked, the platform should have been off.
journal.whatWentWell: I did identify several truths more clearly than before. First, a valid hold that fails is not the same thing as a greedy hold. Second, the revenge sequence after the stopout was the real problem. Third, I articulated a much cleaner management framework: in a 10-minute downtrend, take the 1R scalp; once 10-minute momentum truly shifts and holds, then go for 1R first and allow room for 2R+. I also told myself something important before the session completely fell apart: the market will be here forever and there will be more chances. That voice was right even if I did not obey it soon enough.
journal.whatToImprove: The big one is immediate shutdown once emotional hijack is recognized. If I say out loud that I am hijacked, I should not be allowed to click again. I also need to anchor management to the 10-minute chart much more consistently. 1-minute urgency cannot keep overriding the bigger context. Finally, I need a hard cap on “repair mode” behavior. If I catch myself thinking about getting money back instead of trading the actual chart, that is a no-trade signal, not a reason to look harder for entries.
journal.lessonLearned: The emotional side is still the biggest source of loss. Today showed that even when I can diagnose the pattern accurately, diagnosis alone is not enough unless it changes behavior in real time. The better trading rule is now clearer: 10-minute downtrend means scalp longs and take 1R, no over-negotiation. 10-minute reversal and hold means first target at 1R, then give the rest room for 2R+. But none of that matters if I am already hijacked and still clicking. Protecting mental capital must happen before the next trade, not after the day is already wrecked.
journal.nextRule: If I say I am emotionally hijacked, platform off. No more trades. Also: manage from the 10-minute chart. Against 10-minute trend, take the 1R scalp. With a real 10-minute momentum shift and hold, take 1R first and only then allow the trade to try for 2R+.

journal.title: Trading Journal — Monday, April 27, 2026
journal.mood: Shaken early, defensive through much of the morning, but more self-aware than Friday. I was not happy with the process, especially the way early reversals triggered leftover PTSD from the prior 1R-to-BE experiences, but I also did something important: I chose to stop with a small gain instead of letting the day become another emotional disaster.
journal.setupContext: Today reinforced that old pain can carry forward even when I think I am over it. The early market action kept recreating the exact kind of reversal pattern that has been bothering me lately, and that made it harder to trust otherwise valid setups. The chart lesson remains the same: 10-minute and 5-minute context have to anchor the trade. If I let recent emotional memory override present structure, I become reactive, not analytical. The better setups later in the day still came from clearer structure and better patience, but they were harder to hold and manage because the emotional residue from the morning was already there.
journal.execution: There were 23 trades today, which is still too many and reflects state instability. Early sequence: -$51.50, -$53.50, +$16.50, -$51.00, -$50.00, -$5.00 before the rebound trade of +$51.00. That early stretch set the emotional tone. Later there were several small wins, a couple larger winners like +$33.00 and +$91.00, and some middling scratches/losses, but the trade count shows that I kept searching too hard for stability after the early shock. The day did not become a total disaster because I finally accepted that the process was off and chose to finish with a small gain rather than keep pressing. Total gross: +$46.50.
journal.psychology: Recovery Metrics: mixed, but materially better than Friday because I did eventually regain enough awareness to stop. The most important emotional insight was that the early reversals triggered leftover PTSD from recent 1R-to-BE reversals. That means I was not just trading the chart, I was trading memory and fear. Once that happens, confidence gets shaky and even good setups start feeling unsafe. The positive is that I could see the pattern in real time and tell myself to take the small green and reset for tomorrow. That is still progress, even if the day itself felt messy.
journal.ruleAdherence: Mixed to weak. The total number of trades says that I was still letting state drive too many decisions. I did not stay as disciplined as I want around selectivity once the early sequence hit. On the positive side, I did obey a more important higher-level rule late in the session: I stopped instead of trying to force emotional satisfaction. That choice probably saved the day from turning into another Friday-style spiral.
journal.whatWentWell: I did not let a shaky process day turn into a catastrophic process day. I had enough awareness to identify the specific emotional trigger, early reversals recreating the recent 1R/BE pain, and I listened to the better voice that said the market will be here tomorrow. I also still found and executed some valid trades later in the day, which shows the edge does not disappear, it just gets harder to access when my state is off.
journal.whatToImprove: I need a more explicit rule for PTSD-pattern days. If the first few trades reproduce the exact emotional wound that has been bothering me lately, I should reduce decision load fast instead of continuing to take so many attempts. I also need to trust that stopping with a small gain or small loss is a win when the process is deteriorating. Finally, I want fewer trades. Even when the day ends green, 23 trades means the emotional side had way too many opportunities to interfere.
journal.lessonLearned: Old emotional wounds can still shape new trading days if I do not actively guard against them. Today proved that recent 1R-to-BE reversals are still sitting in my system and can distort how I read risk, confidence, and follow-through. The right response is not to power through that state with more trades. The right response is to notice it early, reduce exposure to bad decisions, and protect the next day. Ending with a small profit on a messy process day is better than proving I can survive emotional turbulence by brute force.
journal.nextRule: If the morning recreates the exact emotional pain pattern that has been affecting me lately, reduce decision load immediately. Fewer trades, more emphasis on 10-minute/5-minute context, and if confidence becomes defensive instead of clear, take the small green or small loss and reset for the next trading day.

journal.title: Trading Journal — Tuesday, April 28, 2026
journal.mood: Ashamed, overamped, and out of rhythm. Even though the day finished green, I do not feel good about it because I know the green came from breaking structure, sizing rules, and discipline instead of from clean execution.
journal.setupContext: The chart context did not justify the way I traded. There were real opportunities during the session, but I did not engage them with a clean framework. Instead, after early losses and more emotional pressure, I shifted into reactive mode. I started taking too many attempts and then layered in larger size. That means the issue was not a lack of setups. It was lack of state control and lack of adherence to my current stage. The market did not force 2 contracts or 35 trades on me. I did that.
journal.execution: There were 35 trades total. Early sequence was ugly: -$8.00, -$50.00, -$51.00, +$67.00, -$50.00, -$52.00 before a series of choppy scalps and more losses. The biggest red flag is that 12 of the 35 trades were taken with 2 contracts even though I am not approved for 2-contract live trading yet. Later in the session, a few 2-contract winners such as +$160.00, +$76.00, +$77.00, +$71.00 and other green trades helped recover the P&L. But that does not rescue the process. It only shows that uncontrolled size and frequency can still stumble into green days, which is exactly the trap. Total gross: +$171.50.
journal.psychology: Recovery Metrics: poor. I was not centered. The emotional side clearly had too much authority, and once that happened I started acting like P&L itself could validate the day. That is the dangerous lie. A green day can still be a bad trading day if it comes from broken rules. I could feel that while trading and I definitely feel it now. The shame is not from losing. It is from knowing I did not respect the version of trader I am trying to become.
journal.ruleAdherence: Weak. I broke the current stage rule by trading 2 contracts. I violated the spirit of the max-trades and process-first discipline by taking 35 trades. I allowed outcome-based thinking to blur the fact that this was not earned cleanly. The only meaningful positive is that I can recognize all of this clearly now instead of hiding behind the green number.
journal.whatWentWell: I am not lying to myself about the day. That matters. I can see that green P&L does not automatically equal good process. I also did still identify and capture some real moves during the day, which means the underlying market-reading ability is not absent. The problem was that I wrapped some valid reads inside bad state, bad frequency, and premature size escalation.
journal.whatToImprove: Go back to 1 contract only, no exceptions. No discretionary size-up because a setup feels obvious or because I want to recover earlier losses. I also need a hard stop on trade count and state degradation. Once I notice that I am trading too frequently or trying to brute-force control back into the day, I need to step away before the next click. Most importantly, I need to stop giving myself emotional credit for a green outcome when the process was weak.
journal.lessonLearned: A green day can absolutely be a loss if it reinforces bad habits. Today showed me that the most dangerous outcome is not always a red day. Sometimes it is a green day that whispers, “See, the rule breaking worked.” I cannot let that become the lesson. The right lesson is the opposite: if I have to break stage rules, overtrade, and size up early to make money, then the process is failing even if the account is up.
journal.nextRule: Back to 1 contract only. No 2-contract trading until the promotion checklist is actually earned. If I break that, the day is already off track. Also: fewer trades, more selectivity, and judge the day by process quality first, P&L second.

journal.title: Trading Journal — Thursday, April 30, 2026
journal.mood: Calm, patient, and more in sync with the market. I felt good waiting for setups instead of forcing entries, and I’m happy with the cleaner 2R executions.
journal.content:
Mood: Calm, patient, and more in sync with the market. Felt good waiting for setups instead of forcing entries.
Recovery Metrics: Better patience today. Emotional control was solid. No revenge trading after the one red trade.
Setup Context: Started with short scalps, then found better reversal setups after flushes. Best signals came from 10m doji candles and 5m hammer/doji candles showing buyers stepping in.
Execution: 9 trades, +$275.50 gross, +$266.50 net after modeled commissions, 8 winners and 1 loser. Best trade was +$100. The main mistake was taking TP off mid-trade to see if it could run further, then changing my mind and closing it anyway.
Psychology: I stayed more patient and waited for confirmation. I read the reversal context well after the market showed its hand. I still felt the urge to optimize mid-trade, which is something I need to cut out.
Rule Adherence: Did well waiting for setups, using confirmation candles, and recognizing buyer response after flushes. Broke discipline by removing TP mid-trade and traded more than my ideal daily cap.
What I Did Good: Waited for the setup. Read reversal patterns well. Used 10m and 5m confirmation effectively. Captured clean 2R trades and stayed in control.
What To Improve: Stick to 1R/2R without changing the plan mid-trade. Reduce trade count. Keep treating doji/hammer candles as alerts, then wait for confirmation before entry.
Lesson Learned: My edge today was waiting for confirmation after the flush, not predicting. When I keep the trade plan simple, I trade better. Removing TP adds emotion and weakens the process.
Next Trade Rule: If the plan is 1R/2R, leave it alone and let it work. Doji is the alert, confirmation is the entry. No mid-trade TP changes unless planned before entry.
Session Summary: Strong green day with better patience and solid pattern recognition. Best trades came from spotting flushes, then waiting for buyers to confirm reversal. The next level is same patience, fewer trades, and stricter 2R discipline.
journal.setupContext: The best reads came after flushes where sellers looked exhausted and buyers clearly stepped in. I specifically noticed two doji candles on the 10-minute chart pointing to possible reversal and also saw two hammer/doji candles on the 5-minute after a flush. That pattern — flush first, then buyer response, then candle confirmation — was the real edge. The setup worked best when I treated doji/hammer candles as alerts and waited for actual confirmation before entry.
journal.execution: I took 9 total trades and closed +$275.50 gross. Early short scalps worked, and the cleaner reversal trades later in the session produced some of the best results. Several trades reflected the 2R structure well, including a +$94.50 trade and a +$100.00 trade. The main execution mistake was removing take profit on one trade to see how far it could go, then changing my mind and taking profit anyway. That moment matters because it turned a clean system trade into a feelings trade, even though the day still ended green.
journal.psychology: My emotional control was much better today. I waited more, forced less, and let the market show its hand before acting. I read the reversal context well once the flushes happened and buyers stepped in. The remaining psychological leak is the urge to optimize a winner in real time by changing TP mid-trade. That impulse is still emotion wearing a smart disguise. Today confirmed that I trade better when I keep the plan simple and do not freestyle once I am in the position.
journal.ruleAdherence: Better than usual, but not fully clean. I did well waiting for setups, using confirmation candles, and recognizing buyer response after the flush. I also stayed composed after the one red trade and did not revenge spiral. The rule strain is that I traded 9 times, which is still above the locked max-3-trades preference, and I removed TP mid-trade once. So this was a good process-improvement day, not a perfect discipline day.
journal.whatWentWell: I waited for confirmation instead of guessing. I identified reversal context correctly after the flushes. I used the 10-minute doji and 5-minute hammer/doji signals the right way by pairing them with buyer response. I captured multiple clean winners and proved that structured 2R trading feels much better than random management. Most importantly, I could feel the difference between trading a setup and trading boredom or impulse.
journal.whatToImprove: Stick to the 1R/2R plan without changing TP mid-trade. Reduce total trade count and be more selective even on green days. Keep treating doji and hammer candles as alerts only, not entries by themselves. If the setup is not A+, let it go. The next level is the same patience and same confirmation quality, but with fewer total decisions.
journal.lessonLearned: My edge today was not prediction — it was waiting for confirmation after the flush. The buyer response mattered more than the dump itself. When I keep the trade plan simple and structured, I trade better. Removing TP adds emotion and weakens the process. Right now, consistency matters more than squeezing extra distance out of a winner.
journal.nextRule: If the plan is 1R/2R, leave it alone and let it work. Doji is the alert, confirmation is the entry. No mid-trade TP changes unless that rule exists before the trade is entered.

journal.title: Trading Journal — Friday, May 1, 2026
journal.mood: Finished green, but more importantly caught the real execution leaks in real time. Protected mental capital and shut it down intentionally before the weekend.
journal.content: Session summary: 8 trades logged. Gross PnL 115.00, commissions 8.00, net PnL 107.00. Win/Loss/BE = 5/3/0 (Winrate 62.5%). Best trade: CSV-20260501-461767407669 (MNQM6) 61.50. Worst trade: CSV-20260501-461767407466 (MNQM6) -51.00.
journal.setupContext: Mixed intraday tape with a broader downtrend context later in the session. Some valid scalp opportunities existed, but lower-timeframe candles inside chop repeatedly tested discipline and made it easy to confuse alert candles with actual reversal confirmation.
journal.execution: Early management leak showed up as a fear-based short exit after a hammer/doji in a prior chop zone. A later countertrend long was cleaner because the 10m 8EMA target was defined before entry as a scalp against the broader downtrend. The weakest trade of the day came from re-entering too quickly after a loss on a doji-and-hope thesis without proof of momentum shift.
journal.psychology: Biggest win was honesty. I identified the difference between structure-based exits and discomfort exits, and I recognized when a trade idea was being fueled by hope rather than confirmation. Stopping for the day was the right call to protect both the account and Monday’s mental state.
journal.ruleAdherence: Mixed but improving. I stayed self-aware enough to catch fear exits and revenge-adjacent behavior in real time, but I still violated process by taking a reversal idea without confirmation shortly after a loss. I finished strong by respecting a predefined scalp target and shutting down intentionally.
journal.whatWentWell: Called out the real reasons behind entries and exits instead of rationalizing them. Respected a preplanned 10m 8EMA scalp target on the final trade. Protected the weekly momentum by ending the session once mental quality started to matter more than squeezing extra P&L.
journal.whatToImprove: Do not let one candle override structure. Do not re-enter within minutes of a loss on the same idea unless momentum shift is clearly proven. Keep higher-timeframe location in control so 1m fear does not make the decision for me.
journal.lessonLearned: A doji is an alert, not a trigger. Discomfort is not an exit signal. When I feel urgency after a loss, the proof requirement must go up, not down.
journal.nextRule: Doji + hope = no trade. After any stop-out on the same idea, wait at least 3 minutes and require clear momentum-shift confirmation before re-entry.

journal.title: Trading Journal — Monday, May 4, 2026
journal.mood: Emotional day because I was close to passing the eval and felt the pressure build after a strong start. The frustration wasn’t from being lost — it came from seeing multiple trades go about 1R and then reverse before I could protect them. That sequence pushed me over the edge and changed the quality of my decision-making.
journal.content: Session summary: 27 trades logged. Gross PnL 77.50, commissions 27.00, net PnL 50.50. Win/Loss/BE = 12/15/0 (Winrate 44.4%). Best trade: CSV-20260504-461767407719 (MNQM6) 100.00, tied with CSV-20260504-461767408034 (MNQM6) 100.00. Worst trade: -50.50, repeated three times.
journal.setupContext: The session started with real opportunity and a clean read. The first trade worked immediately and the first three trades put me in a good position. After that, the market kept offering moves that would reach about 1R and then reverse sharply, which made trade management feel reactive instead of planned. The issue was less about lacking setups and more about handling extension entries and known reaction levels without a predefined 1R decision.
journal.execution: Execution split into two different days. Early on I traded well enough to get traction and had +125.00 after the first three trades. Then the day turned into churn: repeated entries, several full-stop losses, and too many attempts to win back the eval through activity. The biggest execution leak was not having a preplanned 1R management rule once the early rhythm changed. Without that, trades that briefly worked started feeding frustration instead of confidence.
journal.psychology: The emotional break came after multiple trades went roughly 1R and snapped back before I could move stop to breakeven. That created a feeling of almost having it, over and over, and that feeling pushed me into pressing. Once I was trading the eval and the frustration instead of the setup, the market stopped being the main problem. Today was a discipline failure under emotional pressure, not proof that I can’t trade.
journal.ruleAdherence: Poor overall. I respected the market well enough to build an early cushion, but I violated the locked process badly afterward. Twenty-seven trades is far beyond the preferred cap, and the day became reactive after the third trade. I did not maintain a process-first posture once the repeated 1R reversals started to sting.
journal.whatWentWell: Started the session with real edge and proved I can read and capture a clean move. Stayed observant enough after the fact to identify the exact trigger that pushed me off-center. Caught the real lesson: the combination of trade count, emotional pressure, and undefined 1R management was what turned a close eval day into a churn day.
journal.whatToImprove: Define the 1R management decision before entry, especially on later-session or extended setups. Treat three trades as an automatic checkpoint instead of a suggestion. Stop trying to pass the eval through volume once emotional friction appears. When trades start hitting near 1R and reversing, respond with a rule-based pause rather than another entry.
journal.lessonLearned: Being close to the eval can be just as dangerous as being down because the pressure makes every reversal feel personal. When repeated almost-wins start stacking, the solution is not more clicking — it is a forced reset and a predefined management rule.
journal.nextRule: After 3 trades or after 2 trades that reach about 1R and then reverse, stop live trading for 15 minutes. The next trade is allowed only if it is A+ and the 1R management decision is defined before entry.
